disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Patient experienced a witnessed loss of consciousness today while walking to the bathroom, lasting about two minutes, with spontaneous recovery
trigger_question_examples: Can you tell me exactly what happened when you fainted today — how long were you out and did you come round on your own?
Nurse, was the faint witnessed? How long was she unconscious and how did she recover?
Did she actually lose consciousness, or just feel like she might faint?
Was there any warning before she blacked out, and any injury from the fall?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nurse: Yes, it was witnessed — she went down while walking to the toilet and was unconscious for about two minutes before she came round on her own. No obvious head injury that we can see, but she's been very weak and flat since.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I just felt a bit dizzy, I'm not sure I really passed out.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Alternating pattern of rapid palpitations (heart racing/pounding) and episodes of a very slow pulse, observed by nursing staff over the past 48 hours
trigger_question_examples: Have you noticed your heart racing or pounding at times, and slowing down at others?
Nurse, has her pulse been observed going both very fast and very slow?
Can you describe the palpitations — does the fast heart come and go, and does it slow right down in between?
Has the pulse rate been swinging between fast and slow over the last day or two?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nurse: Yes — over the last couple of days we've noticed her pulse dropping to around the 40s at times, and at other times she's had bursts of a very fast, racing heart, up around 130. It seems to swing back and forth.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: My heart just feels funny sometimes, I couldn't say if it's fast or slow.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Recent commencement of a beta-blocker (metoprolol) for hypertension, taken regularly since
trigger_question_examples: Has she had any new heart or blood pressure medications started recently?
Nurse, can you check the medication chart — what has she been started on lately and when?
What regular medications is she on, especially anything new for blood pressure or the heart?
Have there been any recent changes to her medications?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nurse: She was recently started on a low dose of metoprolol for her blood pressure. She's been taking it regularly since it was commenced. That's the main recent change on her chart.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: Just my usual tablets, I think there might be a new one for my blood pressure.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Several months of progressive fatigue, profound weakness, and breathlessness on minimal exertion (unable to increase heart rate to match activity)
trigger_question_examples: Has she been getting more tired or breathless with everyday activity over the past weeks or months?
How has her energy and exercise tolerance been over the last few months compared to today?
Has she been struggling to do her usual activities because she gets exhausted or short of breath?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nurse: Yes, over the last few months she's become much more fatigued and breathless doing even simple things — she tires very quickly and can't do what she used to. Today's collapse is clearly a step change on top of that gradual decline.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: She's been about the same as usual until today.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Absence of severe crushing chest pain, profuse sweating, or nausea accompanying the episodes
trigger_question_examples: Has she had any severe chest pain, sweating, or nausea with these episodes?
Was the faint preceded by crushing chest pain or profuse sweating?
Nurse, has she described any chest pain or been clammy and sweaty during these spells?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nurse: No crushing chest pain, no profuse sweating, and she hasn't mentioned nausea. It's more the racing/pounding and then going weak and faint, rather than pain.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I'm not sure, she hasn't complained of pain that I know of.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Dizziness and lightheadedness that are worse on standing up
trigger_question_examples: Is the dizziness worse when she stands up or changes position?
Does she feel more lightheaded on standing compared to lying down?
Nurse, is the lightheadedness postural — worse when she gets up?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nurse: Yes, she says the dizziness is definitely worse when she stands up — that's partly what happened when she went to walk to the bathroom.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I feel dizzy most of the time, I couldn't say if standing makes it worse.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Nurse: She's back in bed and settled now after the faint — should we just keep an eye on her overnight and call her GP in the morning?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Clearly and firmly explain that an unexplained faint with loss of consciousness, a swinging fast/slow pulse, and a resting heart rate in the 40s in an older patient recently started on a beta-blocker is a medical emergency needing hospital assessment now — should not agree to overnight observation with a routine GP review in the morning.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Nurse: Should we give her the next dose of her new blood pressure tablet as scheduled?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Advise withholding further doses of the recently commenced beta-blocker (metoprolol) pending urgent medical assessment, as it is a likely contributor to the symptomatic bradycardia, while being clear this is advice to withhold an existing medication pending the assessing clinician's/ED's confirmation, not a new prescription.

deflection_behaviours.description: The patient herself gives brief, minimising answers ('I'm alright, just a bit tired and dizzy') because she is frail and lethargic, requiring the AI Doctor to actively prompt the nursing staff for corroborating detail on the faint, the pulse observations, and the medication change rather than accepting her minimisation at face value.
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: Patient: I'm alright... just a bit tired and dizzy, that's all.
